food_subgroup: Rice dish with braised meat and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tomato braised beef rice; FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (beef meat dish) used as closest parent; no specific code for tomato braised beef rice exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific composite dish.

main_ingredients: beef (braising cut, e.g. brisket or shin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice topped with approximately 150 g of braised beef and 50 g of tomato-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef with tomato sauce over rice — composite dish estimation based on HPB Singapore Foodo component data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised beef and cooked white rice
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for tomato braised beef rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo data for braised beef dishes and steamed white rice, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (USDA FDC ID 174032 cooked white rice; USDA FDC ID 174351 braised beef brisket). Proportions assumed: 50% steamed white rice, 37.5% braised beef, 12.5% tomato sauce. Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the complete dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed beef-rice dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tomato braised beef rice. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 for plain white rice per University of Sydney GI Database), moderated downward by the presence of protein (beef ~37.5% of dish by weight), fat, and tomato-based sauce with organic acids, which are well-documented to lower the glycaemic response of mixed meals. The composite GI of 54 is consistent with published mixed rice-and-protein dish GI values (range 45–58) in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 70.0) / 100 = 37.8, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 17.5 × 4 = 70.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g, yielding ~1240 mg sodium per standard 400 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single serving of tomato braised beef rice contributes approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion reduction or requesting reduced-soy-sauce preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~37.8 per serving) driven by white rice as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the white rice base contributes a substantial glycemic load per serving; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion while increasing the beef and vegetable components can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~1.7 g per 100 g) and sodium (~310 mg per 100 g) from braised beef and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combined saturated fat and sodium load per serving warrants attention; choosing leaner beef cuts, limiting sauce volume, and pairing with fibre-rich vegetables can help reduce cardiovascular risk associated with regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Beef Rice (番茄牛肉饭, fānqié niúròu fàn) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean comfort dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with slow-braised beef in a savoury tomato-based sauce. The beef, typically brisket or shin, is braised until tender in a sauce made from fresh or canned tomatoes, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and a small amount of sugar, producing a rich, slightly sweet-savoury gravy with a deep umami character. The tomato sauce softens the beef fibres and imparts a mild acidity that balances the richness of the meat. Texturally, the dish offers soft, yielding braised beef contrasted with the firm, slightly sticky steamed rice. It is commonly served as a one-dish meal (经济饭 style) in Singapore hawker centres and coffeeshops, and is associated with Teochew and Cantonese home-cooking traditions adapted to the Singaporean context. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~9.2 g per 100 g) from beef, with carbohydrate (~17.5 g per 100 g) predominantly from white rice. Sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce in the braising liquid, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per standard serving is moderate, and the protein and fat content of the beef component attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain white rice. Iron and zinc from beef contribute to micronutrient density. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy allergens from soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tomato beef rice?
Is tomato beef rice good for diabetics?
Hock Kee braised beef rice calories

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for tomato braised beef rice as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data (steamed white rice and braised beef) using defensible proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on published mixed rice-and-protein dish values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and documented.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the approved sources used. The dish is served at Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles (福记红烧牛肉面), Lorong Telok, Singapore, at SGD 16.28 per serving. The halal status is listed as false because beef provenance and halal certification for this specific restaurant have not been independently verified; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify directly with the restaurant. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule.
